hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, they have largely retained their original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their cross-gabled roofline, scalloped cladding on the gables, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone sills and lintels. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

context.street_character: One-way SB | 30 km/h | Meets minimum standards | Secondary commercial spine

Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles.

facade_description: 11 Kensington Ave presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology. The facade reads primarily as brick, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, they have largely retained their original building features reflecting vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles, including their cross-gabled roofline, scalloped cladding on the gables, and fenestration pattern with flat headed openings featuring stone sills and lintels. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
